explanation: Ohm's law: \( V = IR \). Valid for ohmic conductors at constant temperature. SI unit of resistance: Ohm (Ω).
name: Ohm's Law

text: The resistivity \(\rho\) of a conductor is related to resistance \(R\) by:
explanation: Resistance: \( R = \frac{\rho L}{A} \), where \(L\) = length, \(A\) = cross-sectional area, \(\rho\) = resistivity.
name: Resistivity and Resistance

options: Sum of voltages in a loop = 0
Sum of currents entering a junction = sum of currents leaving (\(\sum I = 0\))
Current is same everywhere in a circuit
Voltage divides in proportion to resistance
explanation: KCL (junction rule): \( \sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out} \). Based on conservation of charge.
name: Kirchhoff's Current Law

options: Current is constant around a loop
Sum of all voltages (EMFs and drops) around any closed loop = 0
Voltage is same across all elements
explanation: KVL (loop rule): \( \sum V = \sum EMF - \sum IR = 0 \). Based on conservation of energy.
name: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

text: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced when:
options: Sum of all resistances is zero
explanation: Wheatstone bridge balance: \( \frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S} \) or \( PS = QR \). Used to find unknown resistance accurately.
name: Wheatstone Bridge Balance Condition

text: The internal resistance \(r\) of a battery can be found using:
explanation: Terminal voltage: \( V = E - Ir \). When external \(R\) is connected: \( I = E/(R+r) \) and \( V = IR = E-Ir \).
name: Internal Resistance

text: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is:
options: The slow average velocity in direction of field: \( v_d = eE\tau/m \)
explanation: Drift velocity: \( v_d = \frac{eE\tau}{m} \approx 10^{-3}\) m/s. Very slow! Current = \( I = nev_d A \).
name: Drift Velocity

text: The potentiometer is used to measure EMF because it draws:
explanation: Potentiometer: null method — at balance, no current flows through galvanometer. Gives accurate EMF without drawing current from source.
name: Potentiometer Principle

text: The power dissipated in a resistor \(R\) carrying current \(I\) is:
explanation: Power: \( P = I^2R = V^2/R = VI \). All three forms are equivalent using \(V=IR\). Unit: Watt.
name: Power Dissipation

text: The temperature coefficient of resistance \(\alpha\) is defined by:
explanation: \( R_T = R_0(1+\alpha\Delta T) \). For metals: \(\alpha > 0\) (resistance increases with T). For semiconductors: \(\alpha < 0\).
name: Temperature Coefficient of Resistance

text: The mobility \(\mu\) of charge carriers is:
options: \( \mu = v_d / E \) (drift velocity per unit electric field)
explanation: Mobility: \( \mu = v_d/E = e\tau/m \). Units: m²/(V·s). Conductivity \( \sigma = ne\mu \).
name: Carrier Mobility

text: For resistors in series, the equivalent resistance is:
explanation: Series: \( R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots \). Current is same through all. Parallel: \( 1/R_{eq} = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 \).
name: Resistors in Series

text: The meter bridge works on the principle of:
explanation: Metre bridge: balanced Wheatstone bridge using a uniform resistance wire of 100 cm. \( R/X = l/(100-l) \) gives unknown resistance X.
name: Metre Bridge

text: Joule's law of heating states heat produced \( H = \)
explanation: Joule's law: \( H = I^2Rt \) (in joules). In calories: \( H = I^2Rt/4.18 \). Basis for electric heaters, fuses.
name: Joule's Law of Heating

text: The EMF of a cell is defined as work done per unit charge to move charge:
options: Through complete circuit (including internal resistance): EMF = W/q
explanation: EMF: work done per unit positive charge through entire circuit (including inside cell) = energy per unit charge supplied by cell. Units: Volt.
name: Definition of EMF
